mnemonics.title: Coffman Conditions Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: M-H-N-C: 'My Horse Nods Calmly' - Memorize the 4 conditions: Mutual exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait

mnemonics.title: Deadlock Handling Methods
mnemonics.content: P-A-D-R: 'Prevent, Avoid, Detect, Recover' - The four strategies to deal with deadlocks

mnemonics.title: Critical Section Rules
mnemonics.content: P-E-N: 'Progress, Exclusion, Bounded Waiting' - Three properties every critical section solution must satisfy

mnemonics.title: Banker's Algorithm Steps
mnemonics.content: S-A-C-R: 'Safety Check, Allocation, Claim, Request' - Steps to determine if a safe sequence exists

mnemonics.title: Synchronization Primitives
mnemonics.content: M-S-C-L: 'Mutex, Semaphore, Condition variable, Lock' - Common synchronization tools

acronyms.usage: Used when only one thread can access a resource at a time. Think: Locking a door
Integer-based counter for managing resource count. P() = wait (decrement), V() = signal (increment)
Visual representation of processes holding and requesting resources. Cycles indicate potential deadlock
Code segment where shared resources are accessed. Must be protected from concurrent access
When outcome depends on timing of interleaved operations. Happens when synchronization fails

visualTips.visualization: Two people trying to pass through narrow corridors in opposite directions, both refusing to move back. Neither can proceed.
Imagine a circle of processes where each holds a resource needed by the next one - like people passing plates around a circular table, each keeping one plate
A person holding a pen while waiting for a pencil, instead of putting down the pen first
A directed graph with processes as circles and resources as squares. Request edge (process→resource) and assignment edge (resource→process)
Banker checking if they have enough cash (resources) before approving all loan requests to ensure no one gets stuck

analogies.analogy: Two cars at an intersection, both waiting for the other to move. Neither can proceed without the other moving first - classic traffic deadlock.
Single-occupancy bathroom - only one person can use it at a time. Others must wait outside until the lock is released.
Parking lot with limited spaces - the count shows available spots. Cars (threads) decrement on entry, increment on exit.
Restaurant rule: no one can order dessert until everyone finishes main course - eliminating 'hold and wait' by requiring all utensils at once.
Banker who only lends money if they know for certain they'll get it back - checking safety before every resource request.
Two people trying to update the same Google Doc simultaneously - whichever saves last overwrites the other's changes unexpectedly.
